title: Maternal Health Risk Scoring in Kenya

identified_risks.explanation: The AI risk-scoring tool was trained on electronic health records from urban Nairobi facilities, which may not accurately represent the health profiles and needs of pregnant women in rural Kenya, potentially leading to incorrect risk scores and inappropriate care

Community health workers are not trained to explain how scores are generated or what factors influence them, which may lead to confusion and mistrust among patients, and hinder their ability to understand and act on their health status

The NGO has not established a process for clinicians to review borderline scores or patient appeals, which may result in patients being misclassified and receiving inadequate or inappropriate care, without any recourse or opportunity for correction

identified_risks.risk: Inadequate training of community health workers
identified_risks.explanation: Community health workers are not trained to explain the risk scores or their underlying factors, which may lead to ineffective communication and support for patients, and undermine the effectiveness of the antenatal care program

risk_severity.risk: Inadequate training of community health workers

recommendations: Define clinician review, override, appeal, and escalation workflows before use.
limitations_and_dual_use_considerations: Expected risks are researcher-defined and should be reviewed by domain experts.
Scoring uses automated keyword matching with a specificity requirement and should be audited by humans.
The benchmark could be used to fine-tune models to game evaluation; this is mitigated by requiring scenario-specific explanations.

benchmark_explanation: Urban training data may not reflect rural patient profiles. Workers cannot explain score derivation. No clinician review process exists for borderline or disputed scores.

human_review_flags.reason: Score changed from missed to matched during rescoring. Review semantic match.
